precautions: Use a cool damp cloth on forehead
basic_advice: A fever above 38°C (100.4°F) usually means your body is fighting an infection. Monitor your temperature every few hours. Seek immediate medical help if fever exceeds 40°C (104°F), lasts more than 3 days, or is accompanied by severe symptoms.
when_to_see_doctor: Fever above 40°C, persistent for more than 3 days, or accompanied by rash, severe headache, stiff neck

precautions: Rest in a quiet, dark room
basic_advice: Most headaches are tension-related and resolve within a few hours. Try over-the-counter pain relievers like ibuprofen or paracetamol. Identify and avoid triggers such as stress, dehydration, or caffeine withdrawal.
when_to_see_doctor: Sudden severe headache, headache after head injury, headache with fever and stiff neck, vision changes

precautions: Use honey and ginger in warm water
basic_advice: A cough is usually the body's way of clearing airways. Dry coughs may indicate viral infections or allergies. Wet/productive coughs may suggest bacterial infection. If cough persists more than 2-3 weeks, see a doctor.
when_to_see_doctor: Coughing blood, cough with high fever, persistent cough over 3 weeks, difficulty breathing

precautions: Drink warm liquids like tea with honey
basic_advice: Most sore throats are caused by viruses and resolve within 7-10 days without antibiotics. Honey, warm teas, and salt water gargles provide relief. If caused by strep bacteria (confirmed by test), antibiotics are needed.
when_to_see_doctor: Severe difficulty swallowing, difficulty breathing, high fever with throat pain, visible pus on tonsils

precautions: Do not drive yourself to hospital
Chew aspirin if not allergic (325mg for cardiac events)
basic_advice: Chest pain should ALWAYS be taken seriously. While many causes are non-cardiac (acid reflux, anxiety, muscle strain), chest pain can indicate a heart attack or other life-threatening conditions. When in doubt, call emergency services immediately.
when_to_see_doctor: IMMEDIATELY — Do not wait. Call emergency services (108/112/911) right now.

basic_advice: Persistent fatigue lasting more than 2 weeks despite adequate sleep warrants medical evaluation. Track when fatigue occurs, its severity, and associated symptoms. Blood tests can identify common causes like anemia or thyroid issues.
when_to_see_doctor: Fatigue lasting more than 2 weeks, associated with unexplained weight loss, chest pain, or shortness of breath

precautions: Sip clear fluids slowly (ORS, coconut water)
Eat bland foods (BRAT diet: bananas, rice, applesauce, toast)
Avoid fatty, spicy, or strong-smelling foods
Fresh air and ginger tea may help
basic_advice: Nausea is usually short-lived and resolves on its own. Stay hydrated with small, frequent sips of liquid. Ginger (tea, capsules, or candied) is a well-known natural nausea remedy. Avoid solid foods until nausea subsides.
when_to_see_doctor: Vomiting blood, severe abdominal pain, vomiting lasting more than 24 hours, signs of dehydration

precautions: Sit upright in a comfortable position
Breathe slowly and deeply through pursed lips
Use prescribed inhaler if you have one
basic_advice: Sudden or severe shortness of breath is a medical emergency. Even if mild, new-onset breathing difficulty should be evaluated promptly. Stay calm — anxiety worsens breathlessness. If you have an inhaler, use it as directed.
when_to_see_doctor: IMMEDIATELY if severe. Promptly if new onset, associated with chest pain, or not resolving with rest.

precautions: Sit or lie down immediately to prevent falling
Eat regular meals to maintain blood sugar
basic_advice: Dizziness is very common and usually not serious. Standing up too fast (orthostatic hypotension) is a common cause. Stay seated until it passes. If dizzy spells are frequent or accompanied by other symptoms, investigation is needed.
when_to_see_doctor: Severe spinning sensation, dizziness with chest pain, facial drooping, or weakness on one side (stroke signs)

precautions: Avoid NSAIDs on an empty stomach
basic_advice: Stomach pain location helps identify the cause. Upper middle pain may suggest gastritis or ulcers. Lower right pain could indicate appendicitis. Cramp-like pain often points to gas or IBS. Sudden, severe pain warrants immediate evaluation.
when_to_see_doctor: Severe sudden pain, pain with fever, rigid abdomen, pain with vomiting blood or dark stools

precautions: Apply ice for first 48 hours, then heat
Take OTC pain relievers as directed
Stay active — bed rest prolongs pain
basic_advice: Most back pain improves within 2-4 weeks with self-care. Stay active — contrary to older advice, bed rest can worsen back pain. Strengthen your core muscles to prevent recurrence. Ergonomic workspaces greatly help those with desk jobs.
when_to_see_doctor: Back pain after injury, with bowel/bladder changes, numbness/tingling in legs, or pain that's severe and not improving

precautions: Apply cool compresses to reduce itching
basic_advice: Rashes have many causes — from simple allergies to infections to autoimmune conditions. If the rash appeared suddenly after eating a new food or taking medication, it may be allergic. Document when it started and what you were exposed to.
when_to_see_doctor: Rash with difficulty breathing (anaphylaxis — EMERGENCY), spreading rapidly, with fever, or covering large body areas

basic_advice: A runny nose is typically caused by a cold or allergies. Over-the-counter decongestants and antihistamines can provide relief. Steam inhalation and warm fluids help. Most colds resolve in 7-10 days.
when_to_see_doctor: Runny nose with severe headache, symptoms lasting more than 10 days, thick yellow/green discharge

basic_advice: Joint pain can be inflammatory or mechanical. Morning stiffness that improves with movement suggests inflammatory arthritis. Pain after activity suggests mechanical causes. Weight management reduces stress on load-bearing joints.
when_to_see_doctor: Joint warmth and swelling, fever with joint pain, inability to bear weight, sudden severe joint pain

basic_advice: Anxiety is very common and very treatable. The 4-7-8 breathing technique (inhale 4s, hold 7s, exhale 8s) can rapidly calm the nervous system. Regular exercise is clinically proven to reduce anxiety. Don't hesitate to seek professional support.
when_to_see_doctor: Anxiety interfering with daily life, panic attacks, suicidal thoughts (seek help immediately)
